segments.title: German torpedo boats evolved into fleet weapons
segments.summary: The lecture argues that German torpedo boats stayed torpedo-centered while growing from coastal protection craft into larger fleet attack assets.
segments.body: The discussion captures the episode's organizing argument. Dr. Clarke traces Kaiserliche Marine torpedo boats from cheap coastal and port-protection assets through larger divisional and ocean-going boats, then explains why German strategy still emphasized torpedo attack rather than balanced gun-and-torpedo destroyer work. The late move toward V25s and other large boats clarified what the fleet needed, but arrived too late to erase Jutland-era availability and support problems.

evidence.note: The opening frames the question as why Germany stayed with torpedo boats rather than straightforward destroyers and says the answer combines coastal, divisional, and larger-ship development.
The early torpedo-boat section describes the first Kaiserliche Marine approach as coastal and port protection rather than fleet work.
The late-war transition section ties V25s and other large boats to the strategy of using torpedoes for decisive attack, then notes that the realization came too late.
The conclusion defines the distinction: these torpedo boats remain built around torpedoes, while destroyers put more weight on gun armament alongside torpedoes.
